title: Weldment Evaluation: Methods and Discontinuities
narrationScript: Before you can read a weld radiograph, you have to be able to read the weld. Every discontinuity on the film is a moment where the welding process lost control, and each process loses control in its own characteristic way. So this lesson is a working tour of how welds are made, what goes wrong, and where each problem lives in the joint. Start with the anatomy. A typical groove weld joins two beveled plates or pipe ends. The prepared faces are the bevels, the flat land at the bottom of each bevel is the root face, and the space between them is the root gap or root opening. The weld is built in passes. The root pass closes the gap at the bottom. The second pass, called the hot pass in pipe work, follows it. Fill passes build up the groove, and the cap pass finishes the top. Beside the weld metal is the heat affected zone, parent metal whose structure was changed by welding heat. It matters because some of the worst cracking lives there, not in the weld itself. Now the processes. Shielded metal arc welding, stick welding, melts a flux coated electrode. The flux becomes a protective slag layer that must be chipped and ground off between passes. Miss some, and the next pass traps it. So stick welds are candidates for slag inclusions. Flux cored arc welding and submerged arc welding also run on flux, so they share the slag risk. Submerged arc runs hot, smooth, and automatic, and when it goes wrong it tends to go wrong consistently, in long repeating defects. Gas metal arc welding, solid wire MIG, uses shielding gas and makes no slag. Its danger is colder and sneakier. In short circuit transfer the arc can be too cold to melt the side wall, and the new metal just lies against it without fusing. That is lack of fusion, a tight planar defect that radiography can struggle with. Gas tungsten arc welding, TIG, is the precision process, clean and controlled, the standard root pass for critical pipe. Its signature flaw is the tungsten inclusion, a chip of the electrode left in the puddle when the welder dips it. Tungsten is far denser than steel, and we will see next lesson that it prints light, not dark. Now walk the discontinuity catalog with origin and orientation in mind. Porosity is trapped gas, from moisture, contamination, or lost shielding. It can be scattered, clustered, aligned along a pass, or drawn out into piping or wormhole shapes. Slag inclusions ride between passes and along the groove walls. A special form, wagon tracks, comes from the undercut grooves alongside the root pass that did not get ground out. They run parallel to the weld axis on both sides of the centerline. Incomplete penetration means the root never fused through. Its cause is mechanical. Root gap too tight, root face too thick, current too low, electrode too large. It leaves unfused land edges at the root centerline, straight and regular. Lack of fusion is the side wall version, weld metal lying against bevel or previous pass without melting it, a tight plane along the fusion face. Undercut is a groove melted into the parent metal at the toe of the cap or the root, from too much current or travel speed. Underfill means the welder simply did not fill the groove flush. On the other side of the ledger, excessive reinforcement and excessive penetration mean too much metal, the drip through forms called icicles, and burn through, where the arc blew a hole in the root. Root concavity, suck back, leaves the root surface dished below the pipe wall. Misalignment, high low, is fit up error, not fusion error, but it shows on film and codes limit it. And then cracks, the defects that end arguments. Hot cracks form during solidification, often down the centerline of a contaminated or deeply restrained weld, or as star shaped crater cracks where the welder stopped without filling the crater. Cold cracks, hydrogen cracks, form hours or even days after welding in hard heat affected zones of hardenable steel, fed by hydrogen from damp electrodes or dirty surfaces. That delay is exactly why many codes require the final radiograph to wait, commonly twenty four to forty eight hours after welding for susceptible materials. Orientation ties it together. Porosity is volumetric and shows from any angle. Incomplete penetration sits on the root centerline, vertical through the wall, friendly to a centered beam. Lack of fusion lies along the bevel plane, so it favors a beam angled along that plane. Cracks go where stress sent them, and finding them can take more than one angle. Know the process, and the film stops being a puzzle.
imagePrompt: A pipeline welder kneels at a beveled steel pipe joint laying a glowing root pass with a TIG torch while two radiographers stage a gamma exposure device, collimator, and film cassettes farther down the right-of-way at dusk.

sections.heading: Read the Weld Before the Film
sections.body:
Every weld discontinuity is a moment where the process lost control, and each welding process loses control in its own characteristic way. The interpreter who knows the process can predict the defect list before the film is hung — and challenge an identification that makes no metallurgical sense.

First, the anatomy of a groove weld. The prepared faces are the **bevels**; the flat land at the bottom of each bevel is the **root face**; the space between the parts is the **root gap (root opening)**. The weld is built in **passes**: the **root pass** closes the gap, the **hot pass** (pipe-welding term) follows it, **fill passes** build the groove, and the **cap pass** finishes the surface. Flanking the weld metal is the **heat-affected zone (HAZ)** — parent metal structurally changed by welding heat. The HAZ matters to a radiographer because some of the worst cracking lives there, not in the deposited metal.

sections.heading: Welding Method Review
sections.body:
- **SMAW (stick)** — a flux-coated electrode whose flux becomes a protective **slag** blanket that must be chipped and ground between passes. Missed slag gets trapped by the next pass, so SMAW welds are prime candidates for **slag inclusions**; damp electrodes add **hydrogen** and porosity risk.
- **FCAW (flux-cored)** — wire-fed with flux in the core: high deposition, same slag discipline as stick.
- **SAW (submerged arc)** — automatic, high heat input under a granular flux bed; smooth uniform beads, but when it goes wrong it goes wrong **consistently**, producing long repeating defects (slag lines, centerline cracking in deep single passes).
- **GMAW (solid-wire MIG)** — gas-shielded, no slag. Its danger is colder and sneakier: in **short-circuit transfer** the arc can be too cold to melt the sidewall, leaving **lack of fusion** — a tight planar defect radiography can struggle to show.
- **GTAW (TIG)** — the precision process and the standard root pass for critical pipe; clean and controlled. Signature flaw: the **tungsten inclusion**, a chip of electrode dipped into the puddle. Losing the shielding or purge gas on stainless oxidizes the root — **sugaring**.

sections.caption: Single-V groove weld cross-section mapping where each major discontinuity lives in the joint.

sections.heading: The Weld Discontinuity Catalog
sections.body:
- **Porosity** — trapped gas from moisture, contamination, or lost shielding: scattered, **cluster**, **aligned (linear)**, or elongated **piping / wormhole** forms.
- **Slag inclusions** — flux residue trapped between passes and along groove walls. **Wagon tracks**: parallel slag lines on both sides of the root centerline, left in the undercut grooves beside the root pass that were not ground out.
- **Incomplete penetration (IP)** — the root never fused through: root gap too tight, root face too thick, current too low, electrode too large. Leaves straight unfused land edges at the root centerline.
- **Incomplete (lack of) fusion (LOF)** — weld metal lying against bevel or prior pass without melting it: a tight plane along the fusion face.
- **Undercut** — a groove melted into parent metal at the cap or root toe (excess current/travel speed). **Underfill** — groove not filled flush.
- **Excess reinforcement / excess penetration** — too much metal; drip-through **icicles**; **burn-through** — the arc blew a hole at the root. **Root concavity (suck-back)** — root surface dished below the wall.
- **Misalignment (hi-lo)** — fit-up offset; not a fusion defect, but visible on film and limited by code.
- **Tungsten inclusions**, **arc strikes**, **spatter**, and stainless root **oxidation (sugaring)** round out the list.
- **Cracks** — covered next; they end arguments.

sections.heading: Cracks: Hot, Cold, and Delayed
sections.body:
**Hot (solidification) cracks** form while the weld is solidifying. Sulfur or other contamination and deep, highly restrained beads drive them down the **weld centerline**; an abrupt stop without filling the end crater leaves a star-shaped **crater crack**. They are intergranular and often slightly ragged.

**Cold (hydrogen, delayed) cracks** form in fully solid metal — hours to days after welding — when three ingredients meet: **hydrogen** (damp electrodes, moisture, dirty surfaces), a **hard microstructure** (hardenable steel, fast cooling), and **stress**. They favor the **HAZ**: toe cracks, root cracks, and **underbead cracks** hidden below the weld, and they can also run **transverse** across the weld. The delay is why many codes hold the final radiograph of susceptible materials for **24 to 48 hours after welding** — shoot too early and you certify a weld that has not finished cracking.

For the radiographer, cracks are the highest-stakes call on the film: tight, planar, and never acceptable under the fabrication codes you will work to.

sections.caption: Welding processes and their characteristic discontinuities.
rows: Slag inclusions, wagon tracks, porosity from damp rods, undercut
Long repeating slag lines, centerline hot cracks
Lack of fusion (short-circuit mode), porosity if gas lost
Tungsten inclusions, root oxidation if purge lost

sections.caption: Pass-by-pass view of a groove weld showing which discontinuities enter at each stage.

sections.heading: Origin and Typical Orientation
sections.body:
Tie each discontinuity to an address and an attitude:

- **Root zone, on centerline** — IP, burn-through, root undercut, root concavity. IP stands vertical through the wall on the centerline: friendly to a centered beam.
- **Along the bevels** — slag between passes, wagon tracks paralleling the root, and LOF lying **in the plane of the fusion face**. LOF favors a beam angled along the bevel plane; broadside it can vanish.
- **At the toes and surface** — undercut, underfill, overlap, arc strikes.
- **In the HAZ or weld metal** — toe, root, underbead, and transverse hydrogen cracks, appearing on a delay.
- **At stops and starts** — crater cracks, tie-in slag and porosity.

Most weld discontinuities elongate **parallel to the weld axis**, which is why technique cares about beam orientation relative to the seam — and why a crack that wandered off-axis may take more than one exposure to confirm. Predict from the process, then verify on the film.

sections.title: Every flaw has a process address
sections.body: Slag says flux process and poor interpass cleaning. LOF says cold GMAW sidewall. Tungsten says a dipped TIG electrode. Delayed HAZ cracks say hydrogen plus hardenable steel — and justify the **24–48 hour** hold before final radiography. Identify the process and the film starts answering questions instead of asking them.

figures.title: Single-V groove weld: discontinuity location map
Pass by pass: when each flaw enters

checkQuestions.question: Two parallel slag lines run along both sides of the root centerline of a pipe weld. The most likely origin is:
options: Tungsten electrode dipping during the cap pass
Slag left in the undercut grooves beside the root pass that was not ground out before the hot pass — wagon tracks
Copper pickup from the contact tip
Moisture in the shielding gas
checkQuestions.explanation: Wagon tracks are the classic flux-process defect: the root pass leaves shallow grooves at its toes, and slag trapped there by the next pass prints as parallel lines flanking the centerline.

checkQuestions.question: A structural shop runs GMAW in short-circuit transfer on thick plate. The characteristic discontinuity this setup risks is:
options: Slag inclusions, because solid wire carries flux
Lack of fusion, because the cold arc can fail to melt the sidewall so new metal lies against it unfused
Tungsten inclusions from electrode dipping
Excessive penetration from high heat input
checkQuestions.explanation: Short-circuit GMAW is a low-heat-input mode; on heavy sections the puddle can freeze against an unmelted sidewall, producing tight planar lack of fusion — and GMAW makes no slag at all.

checkQuestions.question: Why do many codes require the final radiograph of hardenable-steel welds to wait 24 to 48 hours after welding?
options: The film needs that long to stabilize after processing
Hydrogen (delayed) cracks in the HAZ can form hours to days after welding, so an early film could certify a weld that has not finished cracking
Hot cracks only become visible after the weld cools to room temperature
The code allows time for slag to settle to the root
checkQuestions.explanation: Cold cracking needs hydrogen, a hard microstructure, and stress, and it develops on a delay — radiographing immediately can miss cracks that grow later, so susceptible materials get a holding period.

checkQuestions.question: Which set of conditions most directly produces incomplete penetration in a single-V groove weld?
options: Root gap too tight, root face too thick, current too low, or electrode too large for the joint
Excessive root gap and excessive current
Damp electrodes and high interpass temperature
Excessive grinding of the hot pass
checkQuestions.explanation: IP is mechanical: the arc never fused the root lands, which happens when the gap or current is too small for the electrode and root-face geometry; excess gap and current produce the opposite — burn-through and icicles.

checkQuestions.question: Lack of fusion along a 30-degree bevel face is suspected. Which beam orientation gives the best chance of detecting it?
options: Any orientation — LOF is volumetric and shows from all angles
Beam perpendicular to the plate surface, broadside to the fusion face
Beam angled so it travels roughly along the plane of the bevel (fusion face)
Beam parallel to the weld axis
checkQuestions.explanation: LOF is a tight planar separation in the plane of the fusion face; it presents detectable path difference only when the beam runs roughly along that plane, which is why broadside shots miss it.

references: CP-105-2024 RT Topical Outline, Radiographic Interpretation and Evaluation Course, IE 4.1–4.3
AWS welding process and discontinuity terminology (general reference)
Fabrication codes' delayed-inspection provisions for hydrogen-crack-susceptible steels (general reference)
